articles.title: Gaza Holds First Election in 20 Years — But Only in One City
articles.summary: Imagine your town hasn't had an election in over 20 years. That's what happened in Gaza. But on April 25, 2026, people in Deir al-Balah lined up to vote for their local council. More than 70,000 people could vote. The election was a small test run to connect Gaza and the West Bank. Voters said they wanted better services like water and electricity. Some materials like ballot boxes were blocked by Israel, so officials used wooden boxes and leftover ink from a vaccination drive. The election is a big step, but many people are still unsure if it will change anything.

articles.title: Navy on a Dangerous Hunt: Clearing Mines from a Super Important Waterway
articles.summary: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea passage where 20% of the world's oil travels. But now it's filled with hidden mines — underwater explosives that can blow up ships. President Trump says the U.S. Navy is clearing them, but experts say it could take six months or more. The mines are hard to find, and even if the Navy says it's safe, shipping companies might not believe them. Iran may have laid mines, but no one knows for sure. The mission is risky and could affect oil prices worldwide.

articles.title: Should the Justice Department Be Able to Hide Its Lawyers' Mistakes?
articles.summary: The U.S. Justice Department wants a new rule that could let it delay or stop investigations into its own lawyers when they do something wrong. Right now, state bar associations — groups that make sure lawyers follow the rules — can investigate federal prosecutors. But the DOJ says these investigations are being used unfairly by political activists. Critics say the rule would let the government avoid accountability. A 1998 law called the McDade-Murtha Amendment requires federal prosecutors to follow state ethics rules, so the new rule might be illegal.
